data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Anthropogenic climate anomaly (volcanic winter or megadrought) triggered agricultural subsistence crises that palace redistributive economies could not buffer, with differential recovery based on irrigation infrastructure", "mechanism": "Palace fiscal overextension under climatic shock: Eastern Mediterranean polities operated extractive tributary economies storing surplus against aristocratic consumption and military expenditure, not peasant subsistence security. A ~1.5°C temperature depression or 20% precipitation reduction (attested in Anatolian tree rings, Dead Sea levels) eliminated the narrow surplus margin; palaces prioritized elite maintenance over grain distribution, triggering peasant flight to pastoralism or highland refuge; Hittite capital moved 5 times in 50 years; Greece fragmented into hyper-localized villages (Early Iron Age); Egypt's basin irrigation decoupled yield from rainfall variability, permitting contraction without collapse", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Mycenaean Greece", "Ugarit", "Levantine interior", "Syro-Hittite successor states"], "excludes": ["Egyptian Delta and Nile valley", "coastal Levantine emporia with maritime grain access"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Palace stores found full at destruction layers", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian famine texts absent from 1200-1150", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "High-resolution climate records show stability 1250-1150", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Pastoralist populations declined contemporaneously", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Draws on Douglass North institutional economics: palaces as commitment devices that failed under covariance risk. Skeptical stance acknowledged via ref to school-1."}
